critique.summary: The article presents Zeroboot, a project offering sub-millisecond virtual machine (VM) sandboxes for AI agents using copy-on-write (CoW) memory forking. It highlights the technology's low spawn latency, memory efficiency, and hardware-enforced isolation via KVM VMs, along with available SDKs and benchmarks.
mainPoints: Zeroboot enables sub-millisecond VM sandbox spawning using copy-on-write memory forking.
Each sandbox is a real KVM virtual machine with hardware-enforced memory isolation.
Benchmarks show Zeroboot has significantly lower spawn latency and memory usage compared to other microsandboxes.
The system uses Firecracker snapshots to create VM templates, enabling fast fork and execution.
SDKs are provided for Python and TypeScript to interact with the sandbox API.
The project is a working prototype but not yet production-hardened.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Zeroboot's performance and architecture are consistent with the information available on the official GitHub repository and related Firecracker and KVM virtualization technologies. The benchmarks and technical approach described align with known capabilities of copy-on-write memory and KVM-based VM snapshots. However, as the project is a prototype, real-world production performance and security guarantees may vary and require further validation.
considerations: The project is currently a prototype and not production-hardened, so stability and security in production environments are not guaranteed.
No independent third-party benchmarks or reviews are cited to verify the performance claims.
The article does not discuss potential limitations such as compatibility with different workloads or overhead in complex scenarios.
Security implications of using VM snapshots and CoW for sandboxing are not deeply analyzed.
The scalability and resource management under heavy concurrent usage need further exploration.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the technical aspects and potential challenges of using sub-millisecond VM sandboxes with copy-on-write memory forking. Key points include concerns about entropy and security, practical use cases, and limitations related to networking and resource usage.

comments.author: cperciva
comments.text:
Don't forget about entropy! You've just created two identical copies of all of your random number generators, which could be very very bad for security.

The firecracker team wrote a very good paper about addressing this when they added snapshot support.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a critical security issue with entropy duplication in VM forking, referencing a known solution, making it highly accurate and relevant.

comments.author: crawshaw
comments.text:
Nice to see this work! I experimented with this for exe.dev before we launched. The VM itself worked really well, but there was a lot of setup to get the networking functioning. And in the end, our target are use cases that don't mind a ~1-second startup time, which meant doing a clean systemd start each time was easier.

That said, I have seen several use cases where people want a VM for something minimal, like a python interpreter, and this is absolutely the sort of approach they should be using. Lot of promise here, excited to see how far you can push it!
comments.analysis: Provides practical experience with similar technology, noting trade-offs between startup time and complexity, which adds valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: diptanu
comments.text: The tricky part of doing this in production is cloning sandboxes across nodes. You would have to snapshot the resident memory, file system (or a CoW layer on top of the rootfs), move the data across nodes, etc.
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies a significant production challenge related to distributed sandbox cloning, contributing a realistic perspective on deployment complexity.

comments.author: vmg12
comments.text:
Does it only work with that specific version of firecracker and only with vms with 1 vcpu?

More than the sub ms startup time the 258kb of ram per VM is huge.
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about compatibility and resource usage, though the claim about RAM usage being huge depends on context and workload.

comments.author: buckle8017
comments.text: This is how android processes work, but it's a security problem breaking some ASLR type things.
comments.analysis: Makes a relevant comparison to Android process handling and points out security implications, though the comment is brief and less detailed.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text: Does this need passthrough or might we be able to leverage PVM with it on a passthrough-less cloud VM/VPS?
comments.analysis: Asks a technical question about passthrough requirements, indicating curiosity but not providing evaluative content.

comments.author: indigodaddy
comments.text:
Your write-up made me think of:

https://codesandbox.io/blog/how-we-clone-a-running-vm-in-2-s...

Are there parallels?
comments.analysis: Makes a relevant connection to a similar technology, though it is more of a prompt for discussion than an analysis.

comments.author: latortuga
comments.analysis: Briefly suggests a similarity to another project but lacks detail or explanation.

comments.author: handfuloflight
comments.text: Can you run this in another sandbox? Not sure why you'd want to... but can you?
comments.analysis: Poses a speculative question about nested sandboxing, showing curiosity but no substantive insight.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.analysis: A meta-comment about merging threads, unrelated to technical discussion or analysis.
